items.q: What is the purpose of a mission statement?
items.correct: To define the business's core purpose and values, guiding decisions and communicating identity to stakeholders — answering 'why we exist'.
items.misconception: A mission statement is a list of financial targets.
items.random_wrong: A mission statement is a description of what products the business sells.
items.why: Mission statements are qualitative and values-driven, not numerical or product-list. Students confuse mission (purpose) with objectives (specific targets) or product range.

items.q: Why does a company's share price change?
items.correct: Share prices reflect investors' expectations about future profits — driven by company performance, sector trends, economic conditions, and market sentiment.
items.misconception: Share prices go up when a company makes more money this year.
items.random_wrong: Share prices are set by the government each morning.
items.why: Markets are forward-looking. A company can report record profits and see shares fall if results were worse than expected. Students miss the expectations-driven nature of pricing.

items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.
The answer examiners want is: Zero. “High” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Fixed cost £400, contribution £2 each. Break-even units?
items.why: The real figure is 200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
